summary: Jeff Atwood argues that micromanagement creates 'zombies' - mindless employees who can only follow orders and lose the ability to make independent decisions. Using Kathy Sierra's diagnostic quiz and insights from Peopleware, he identifies micromanagement as the primary form of 'teamicide' that destroys team cohesion. The post emphasizes that the core issue is trust: managers who don't trust their teams signal this clearly through their actions, inhibiting team formation. Atwood concludes that if you can't demonstrate trust in your team members, you shouldn't be working with them at all.
key_points: Micromanagement transforms capable employees into dependent 'zombies' who can only function under constant supervision
Even small amounts of micromanagement indicate deeper organizational or leadership problems
True autonomy means the freedom to proceed differently from how your manager would - not just the freedom to be right
Defensive management practices like prescriptive methodologies signal distrust and destroy team cohesion
Trust must be actively demonstrated through actions, not just stated as a value
money_quotes: Micromanaging the people you work with is arguably the exact opposite of what a competent team leader or manager should be spending their time doing.
Who the heck wants to work with zombies anyway?
There is no message you can send that will better inhibit team formation.
The only freedom that has any meaning is the freedom to proceed differently from the way your manager would have proceeded.
The right to be right is irrelevant; it's only the right to be wrong that makes you free.
People who feel untrusted have little inclination to bond together into a cooperative team.
If you don't trust the people you work with – and most importantly, actively demonstrate that trust through your actions – should you really be working with them at all?
key_insight: The freedom to make mistakes and proceed differently from your manager is the foundation of both individual autonomy and effective team formation.
